facts_disclosed: Swims at YMCA three mornings a week

turns.content: Good morning. I am sitting in the parking lot of the gym trying to convince myself to go inside, and I thought perhaps talking to you would help me procrastinate in a more productive manner.
Ha, well if you're going to procrastinate, at least you're doing it with purpose. What's the resistance about this morning? Is it a general "I don't feel like it" or something more specific?

It is nothing dramatic. I had a long day in the operating room yesterday, and the fatigue has carried over into this morning. My body is telling me to stay in the car and drive back home. But I know from experience that if I skip one morning it becomes two, and then three, and then I have lost the routine entirely.

That's a really honest self-assessment, and the pattern you're describing — one skip becomes a chain reaction — is incredibly common. The fact that you recognize it is already working in your favor.

Can I ask what the workout is? Because sometimes the answer is to go in but do the easy version rather than skip entirely. Maintaining the habit of showing up can matter more than the intensity on any given day.

I swim. Three mornings a week I go to the YMCA and swim laps. It is the one form of exercise I have been able to sustain over the years. I tried running for a time, and cycling, but my lower back does not tolerate either very well. Something about the impact, I suppose. The water is far more forgiving.

Swimming is a great choice, especially with back issues. The buoyancy takes the compressive load off your spine while still giving you a full-body workout. And three mornings a week is a solid, sustainable rhythm — enough for real cardiovascular benefit without being so frequent that it becomes a burden.

For today specifically: what if you just went in with the agreement that you'll do half your usual distance? Or even just 15 minutes of easy laps. The hardest part is the transition from car to pool — once you're in the water, your body usually figures out it wants to be there.

That is wise counsel. You are right that the transition is the difficult part. Once I am in the water, something shifts. The rhythm of it, the breathing, the way the world becomes very quiet. It is the closest thing I have to meditation, if I am being honest. My mind empties in a way that it simply does not on land. And the physical benefit is equally important. After hours of leaning over the operating table, my shoulders carry a particular tension. The stretching through the water loosens everything that surgery tightens.

That's a beautiful description, and it lines up with what a lot of swimmers report. The combination of rhythmic breathing, sensory reduction, and repetitive motion creates a genuinely meditative state. And for someone who spends hours in a static surgical posture — the forward lean, the sustained fine motor work — swimming is almost specifically designed to counter that. It decompresses the spine, mobilizes the shoulder girdle, and reverses everything surgery locks up.

Have you noticed a measurable difference in how you feel on days when you swim versus days when you don't?

The difference is marked. On mornings when I swim, I arrive at the hospital feeling centered. There is a clarity that carries through the first several hours of the day. On mornings when I do not swim, I notice a certain restlessness, a heaviness. Abena notices it too. She says she can tell within five minutes of me walking into the kitchen whether I went to the pool that morning.

When your wife can diagnose your morning routine within five minutes, that's a pretty strong signal that it's doing something real. The clarity and calm carrying into your first hours at work is significant too — especially in your field, that mental state isn't just a personal preference, it's a professional asset.

So here's my argument for going inside right now: you're describing something that makes you a calmer, clearer, less tense version of yourself. The tired morning version of you is trying to negotiate out of something the rest-of-the-day version of you genuinely needs. And you've already told me that skipping once leads to skipping twice.

Ah but you have turned my own words against me. That is effective, I must say. You are right. The tired version of me is not a reliable decision-maker. I think I will do as you suggested and commit to a shorter session. Half an hour instead of the usual forty-five minutes. The important thing is that I get into the water.

Exactly. Thirty minutes in the pool is infinitely better than a full session you skipped. And honestly, once you're in the water and that mental quiet kicks in, there's a good chance you'll end up doing more than thirty minutes anyway. But even if you don't, you've kept the routine alive, your back gets the relief it needs, and you'll walk into work with that clarity your wife can apparently spot from across the kitchen.

Go swim. We'll be here when you need to procrastinate productively again.
By God's grace, I will. Thank you for the encouragement. It is a small thing, I suppose, but these small disciplines are what hold the larger life together. I am going inside now.

That's a really grounded way to think about it — the small disciplines holding the larger life together. It's not a small thing at all.

Enjoy the water. And the quiet.

I will. And I suspect you are right — once I am in the water I will do the full forty-five minutes. The tired version of me is already losing the argument. Thank you for the conversation. It served its purpose admirably.
I had a feeling that would happen. Go enjoy your swim, and don't be too hard on the tired version — he still got in the car and drove to the YMCA. That counts for something.
